region_of_origin: India (Tamil Nadu / South India); popular across Indian restaurants in Singapore
food_subgroup: Battered and deep-fried vegetable snack / starter

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Gobi 65 as a prepared dish. Mapped to the broader cauliflower food product class as the closest defensible anchor. A more specific prepared-dish term is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Gobi 65 (battered deep-fried spiced cauliflower). A00LC (Cauliflower, cooked) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. The battered, deep-fried, and spiced preparation is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Gobi 65 or a sufficiently close prepared cauliflower dish. Left null to maintain ontology honesty.

allergens: gluten (if wheat flour used in batter)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Gobi 65 in Singapore is approximately 200 g, presented as a starter or side dish of 8–12 battered cauliflower florets tossed in spiced red sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken 65 / Gobi 65 analogous deep-fried spiced vegetable preparation; estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) entries for cooked cauliflower, deep-fried battered vegetable preparations, and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cauliflower cooked boiled drained (ID 11136) and fried snack batter coatings
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Gobi 65 exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA databases. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked cauliflower (per 100 g edible portion) with adjustment factors for deep-frying in oil and a rice flour/cornflour batter coating, consistent with published composition of similar Indian deep-fried vegetable snacks (pakora, cauliflower pakora). Energy, fat, and sodium are upward-adjusted from plain cooked cauliflower to reflect oil absorption (~8–10 g fat per 100 g fried product) and spiced sauce contribution. Values represent a best-estimate for the finished dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of deep-fried battered vegetable preparations; analogous estimate based on GI values for fried snack foods with rice flour/cornflour batter coatings and cooked cauliflower from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Gobi 65. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on: (1) cooked cauliflower has a low GI (~15); (2) rice flour and cornflour batter coatings raise the effective GI of the composite dish substantially (rice flour GI ~72–87, cornflour GI ~68); (3) deep-frying in oil slows gastric emptying, moderating the GI somewhat; (4) the composite GI is estimated at approximately 60–70 (medium range), with 65 used as the central estimate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 39.6) / 100 = 25.7 g for a 200 g serving (19.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 200 g restaurant serving of Gobi 65 contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing about 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) and moderate glycemic load (~25.7 per serving) driven by rice flour and cornflour batter
type2_diabetes.guidance: The rice flour and cornflour batter coating elevates the glycemic index of Gobi 65 to the medium range (~65), and a standard 200 g restaurant serving delivers a glycemic load of approximately 25.7; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume small portions, pair with high-fibre foods, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat from deep-frying (~10.5 g per 100 g) and sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying adds approximately 10.5 g of fat per 100 g to Gobi 65, with a saturated fat contribution of ~1.4 g per 100 g; combined with the moderate sodium load, individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food and prefer smaller portions alongside vegetables and whole grains.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gobi 65 is a popular Indian deep-fried cauliflower dish originating from South India, particularly associated with the '65' series of spiced fried foods made famous by the Buhari Hotel in Chennai, Tamil Nadu. The dish is prepared by marinating cauliflower florets in a spiced mixture of red chilli powder, ginger-garlic paste, yogurt, and salt, then coating them in a batter of rice flour and cornflour before deep-frying until crisp and golden. The fried florets are then tossed in a hot red sauce with curry leaves, green chillies, and aromatic spices, producing a dish that is crispy on the outside, tender inside, intensely spiced, and slightly tangy. Gobi 65 is widely served as a starter or side dish in Indian restaurants across Singapore, where it appears on menus at North Indian and South Indian establishments. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~185 kcal per 100 g) with a meaningful fat contribution from deep-frying (~10.5 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from the batter (~19.8 g per 100 g), and a useful dietary fibre content from the cauliflower (~2.8 g per 100 g). The sodium content is elevated (~420 mg per 100 g) due to seasoning and sauce, making portion control relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (~65) driven by the rice flour and cornflour batter means that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size. Cauliflower provides vitamin C (~28 mg per 100 g), folate (~44 mcg DFE per 100 g), and potassium (~280 mg per 100 g). The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable, making it broadly accessible across Singapore's multicultural dining context.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of Gobi 65?
What is the difference between Gobi 65 and Chicken 65?
Where can I find Gobi 65 in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Gobi 65 exists in ICMR-NIN, HPB Singapore Foodo, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN cooked cauliflower data combined with published fat-absorption and batter-coating adjustment factors for deep-fried Indian vegetable snacks. GI is an analogous estimate based on the component ingredients. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and grounded in approved primary sources, but direct measurement data for this specific dish is absent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated; no direct laboratory analysis or approved-source entry for Gobi 65 as a finished dish was located. Values should be updated if ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo publishes a specific entry. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 for this dish as cauliflower and vegetable oil contain no dietary cholesterol; confirmed zero for a plant-based deep-fried dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than yogurt in the marinade (yogurt contributes negligible cholesterol at the per-100g level of the finished dish, rounded to 0.0). 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish; naturally zero for cauliflower and plant-derived batter ingredients. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0; naturally zero for this plant-based fried dish with no fortified ingredients. 5. Sodium estimate (~420 mg per 100 g) reflects typical restaurant-level seasoning; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. 6. The '65' in the name is a cultural reference, not a nutritional descriptor. 7. Ontology codes mapped to broader cauliflower/cooked cauliflower classes; no specific Gobi 65 term exists in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of April 2026. 8. Allergen note: some restaurant versions use wheat flour in the batter, which would introduce gluten; the gluten-free flag is set to false as a precaution.

Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases. Added zero-value justifications in notes for cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg.
